title: I: Differencing for Stationarity
concept: Integration (I) prepares time series data by differencing consecutive points, removing trends and cycles so the data becomes stable (stationary).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main benefit of a Moving Average (MA) model?

options.text: It uses past prediction errors to smooth out sudden, temporary shocks
It can predict steady long-term upward trends automatically
It calculates the average of all historical data points to make a flat prediction

exercises.explain: Correct! MA models help smooth out short-term random shocks. But what if our data has a steady upward or downward trend? Let's check!

cards.title: The Endless Climb
cards.body: Imagine trying to predict house prices over 20 years. Prices keep climbing up and up due to inflation. If you train a model on data that is always rising, the average and behavior keep changing. Traditional time series models work best when the data sits around a stable average. How do we stabilize a rising trend?

cards.title: What is Stationarity & Differencing?
cards.body: A time series is stationary if its statistical properties (like its average and variance) stay constant over time. To make a trending series stationary, we use differencing (represented by the 'I' for Integration). This means subtracting each data point from the one before it.

cards.title: Altitude vs. Slope
cards.body: Imagine climbing a mountain. If you record your altitude, the numbers keep going up (non-stationary). But if you record the steepness of each step (the difference in height from your last step), the slope values stay relatively stable. Differencing converts altitude (changing) into slope (stable).

cards.title: Stock Prices vs. Daily Returns
cards.body: A stock's price might start at $100 and climb steadily to $150 over a year. The raw prices are non-stationary because of the upward trend. By differencing (subtracting yesterday's price from today's), we get the daily price change (e.g. +$2, -$1, +$0.50). These daily changes bounce around a stable average of zero, making them stationary!

cards.title: Not Losing the Trend
cards.body: You might think differencing destroys the trend data forever. In fact, after the model makes predictions on the stable, differenced data, it performs 'Integration' (the reverse of differencing) to add the trend back in, returning the final forecast in the original scale.

left.text:
Non-stationary.
Values constantly climb.
Average changes over time.
Hard for AR/MA models to predict directly.

right.text:
Stationary.
Values show step-to-step changes.
Bounces around a flat average.
Ideal for model training.

exercises.prompt: What does it mean for a time series to be 'stationary'?

options.text: Its statistical properties, like its average, remain constant over time
The data points never change and remain completely flat
It has a strong, predictable upward trend

exercises.explain: Correct! Stationary data does not show long-term trends or changing variance; it hovers around a constant mean.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Differencing is the process of subtracting consecutive data points to remove trends.
exercises.explain: True! Subtracting yesterday's value from today's value removes the trend, making the data stationary.

exercises.sentence: To model data with a strong trend, we apply ___ to make it stationary.
exercises.explain: Differencing removes trends by looking at step-by-step changes rather than absolute values.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following data series is already stationary?

options.text: Daily temperature changes in a room (bouncing between -0.5 and +0.5 degrees)
The total global population over the last 100 years (constantly rising)
A company's cumulative revenue since its founding (constantly accumulating)

exercises.explain: Yes! Daily temperature changes hover around a stable average of zero, making the series stationary.

exercises.prompt: You want to forecast a city's water consumption, which has been growing steadily every year. If you feed the raw growing consumption data directly into an AR model, what issue is most likely to occur, and how does the 'I' (Integration) step solve it?

options.text: The AR model will struggle because the baseline average keeps shifting. The 'I' step differences the data to remove the growth trend, lets the model forecast changes, and then adds the trend back at the end.
The model will run out of memory. The 'I' step compresses the data to make it fit into CPU cache.
The AR model will predict negative water usage. The 'I' step multiplies all values by -1 to keep them positive.
The model will confuse water with electricity. The 'I' step separates the columns.

exercises.explain: Spot on! Traditional AR and MA models expect a stable (stationary) series. Differencing stabilizes the mean, and the Integration step reverses it later to give us our final growing forecast. Now, let's put AR, I, and MA together!
